name: Jeopardy
tagline: Picture your future self to break out of present-moment self-sabotage.
situations: About to give in to a habit you're trying to break
Putting off something that matters for your future
Coasting when you know you should act
Losing motivation on a long-term goal
summary: Jeopardy confronts the part of you that assumes there will always be more time. You vividly imagine your future self looking back with regret at the chances you wasted — and let that urgency pull you into action now. It's a deliberate antidote to the comfortable illusion that you can start later.
howItWorks: The present self and future self are psychologically distant — research on temporal discounting shows we treat our future selves almost like strangers, which is why long-term goals lose to short-term comfort. Jeopardy collapses that distance by making the future vivid and emotional, recruiting the loss-aversion that motivates us far more strongly than potential gains.

steps.instruction: Picture yourself at the end of your life, looking back at this exact moment of avoidance.
Feel that future self's grief at the time and potential you wasted by not acting.
Let the urgency move you to take the smallest possible action right now.

whenToUse: When you keep deferring something important to 'later'
When a bad habit is about to win again
When you've lost the sense that your choices matter
Paired after Reversal of Desire when avoidance returns
whenNotToUse: When you're already exhausted or burned out — rest is the right move
If it tips into harsh self-criticism rather than motivation
During acute anxiety, where future-focus fuels dread
tips: Keep the future image specific and personal, not abstract
Follow it immediately with one concrete action, however small
Use sparingly — its power comes from not overusing it

evidence.summary: A clinical practice tool consistent with research on temporal discounting and future-self continuity, where vividly connecting to one's future self increases long-term decision-making.
citations: Stutz, P. & Michels, B. (2012). The Tools. Spiegel & Grau.
Hershfield, H.E. (2011). Future self-continuity. Annals of the NY Academy of Sciences.
